sections.heading: Why Litter Tracking Happens and How to Stop It
sections.content:
Litter tracking is the number one daily annoyance for cat owners. Those tiny granules end up in your bed, between couch cushions, and ground into carpets.

Tracking happens because loose litter clings to your cat's paws and fur, then falls off as they walk. The exit point of the litter box determines how much tracking occurs:

**Open pans** (worst for tracking): Cat steps directly from litter onto your floor. Every granule on their paws transfers immediately.

**Single-entry enclosed boxes** (moderate tracking): Cat walks a short distance inside the enclosure before exiting, shaking loose some litter.

**Two-room enclosed boxes** (best for tracking): Cat walks through a clean transition room before exiting. Most loose litter falls off in the second room.

**Top-entry boxes** (best for tracking, limited accessibility): Cat jumps upward to exit, and gravity pulls loose litter back into the box. Not suitable for senior cats or kittens.

sections.heading: Best Overall: Wooden 2-Room Enclosure ($284.99)
sections.content:
**Design: 2 separate rooms** | Tracking reduction: ~60% vs open pan

The two-room design is the most effective tracking solution that remains accessible to all cats — including seniors, kittens, and cats with joint issues.

Your cat enters the litter room through the side entry, uses the box, then walks through the second room before exiting. The transition room acts as a decompression chamber for litter: loose granules fall from paws and fur onto the room's floor instead of yours.

The solid wood floor in the transition room can be wiped clean in seconds — all the tracked litter stays inside the enclosure.

**Tracking reduction results:**
- Open pan: ~200+ granules tracked per use
- Single-entry enclosure: ~80 granules per use
- Two-room enclosure: ~30 granules per use

For the complete cat litter box comparison, see our [best cat litter box guide](/guides/best-cat-litter-box-2026/).

sections.heading: Litter Type and Tracking
sections.content:
The litter you use affects tracking as much as the box design:

**Worst for tracking:**
- Fine-grain clay litter — clings to paws like sand
- Lightweight litter — designed to be lighter, but scatters more easily

**Best for tracking:**
- Medium-grain clumping clay — heavy enough to fall off paws quickly
- Walnut shell litter — larger particles, less paw adhesion
- Crystal (silica) litter — large beads that don't stick to paws

**The tracking equation:** Low-tracking box + low-tracking litter + litter mat = minimal tracking. You need all three components for the best results.

For a full litter type comparison, see our [clumping vs non-clumping guide](/guides/clumping-vs-non-clumping-litter/).

sections.heading: Litter Mats: The Essential Accessory
sections.content:
Even the best low-tracking box benefits from a litter mat placed at the exit point. Mats catch the remaining 10-20% of granules that make it past the box's tracking reduction features.

**What to look for in a litter mat:**
- Double-layer design with honeycomb pattern that traps granules
- Waterproof bottom layer to prevent moisture seeping through
- Large enough to catch both front and side exits (24" x 18" minimum)
- Easy to clean — shake off or vacuum

**Placement:** Put the mat directly at the enclosure's cat hole or exit point. The cat should step onto the mat before touching your floor. For wide enclosures, position the mat along the entire exit side.

For mat recommendations, see our [best litter box mat guide](/guides/best-litter-box-mat/).

sections.heading: Top Entry vs Side Entry for Tracking
sections.content:
**Top-entry boxes** are the gold standard for tracking reduction. When a cat jumps upward to exit, gravity pulls loose litter off their paws and back into the box. Tracking reduction is 80-90% compared to open pans.

**However, top entry has limitations:**
- Senior cats with arthritis can't jump in/out
- Kittens under 6 months may not be strong enough
- Overweight cats may struggle
- Some cats simply refuse to use them

**Side-entry enclosed boxes** are more universally accessible. Every cat can walk through a side hole. Tracking reduction is lower (40-60%) but works for the entire cat population.

**Our recommendation:** If your cat is young, healthy, and athletic — try a top-entry box first. If you have any concerns about mobility, go with an enclosed side-entry design. The two-room enclosure gives you the best tracking reduction within the side-entry category.

For a deeper dive, see our [top entry vs front entry comparison](/guides/top-entry-vs-front-entry-litter-box/).

sections.heading: How to Reduce Tracking From Any Litter Box
sections.content:
Even if you keep your current box, these strategies reduce tracking significantly:

1. **Switch to medium-grain litter.** Smaller = more tracking. Larger grains fall off paws faster.
2. **Add a litter mat at the exit.** Catches 50-70% of escaped granules.
3. **Fill to 4+ inches.** Shallow litter gets kicked out more easily. Deep litter stays in the box.
4. **Trim paw fur.** Long-haired cats track more because litter lodges between toes. A quick trim helps.
5. **Use a covered/enclosed box.** Even a basic hooded box reduces tracking by 30-40%.
6. **Position away from carpets.** If tracked litter lands on hard floor, it's easy to sweep. On carpet, it grinds in.

For a complete tracking solution guide, see our [how to stop litter tracking guide](/guides/how-to-stop-litter-tracking/).

criteria.name: Entry Design
criteria.description: Two-room or top-entry designs offer the best tracking reduction. Side-entry with walk-through is a good middle ground.

criteria.name: Interior Walk Distance
criteria.description: The further your cat walks inside the box before exiting, the more litter falls off. Two-room designs maximize this distance.

criteria.name: Cat Accessibility
criteria.description: Top-entry isn't suitable for all cats. Choose side-entry enclosed designs for senior, overweight, or arthritic cats.

criteria.name: Mat Compatibility
criteria.description: The exit point should allow placement of a litter mat directly outside. Ensure the mat doesn't block the cat hole.

commonMistakes.mistake: Using fine-grain lightweight litter in any litter box
commonMistakes.whyItMatters: Fine, lightweight litter clings to paws and scatters further. Even the best low-tracking box can't compensate for litter designed to be ultra-light. Switch to medium-grain or heavy-grain clumping litter.

commonMistakes.mistake: Placing a litter mat too far from the exit
commonMistakes.whyItMatters: Litter falls off within the first 2-3 steps. If the mat is 12 inches from the exit, most granules land on your floor before the mat. Place it directly against the exit opening.

commonMistakes.mistake: Filling litter too shallow to save money
commonMistakes.whyItMatters: Shallow litter (under 3 inches) gets kicked out when cats dig. This scatters litter outside the box regardless of design. Fill to 4-6 inches for proper containment and clumping.

faq.question: What type of litter box has the least tracking?
faq.answer: Top-entry litter boxes have the least tracking (80-90% reduction), but aren't suitable for all cats. For universal accessibility, two-room enclosed designs reduce tracking by about 60% while working for cats of all ages and mobility levels.

faq.question: Do covered litter boxes reduce tracking?
faq.answer: Yes, covered boxes reduce tracking by 30-40% compared to open pans. The cat has to walk a short distance inside the cover before exiting, which shakes loose some litter. Two-room designs and top-entry boxes perform even better.

faq.question: Does the type of litter affect tracking?
faq.answer: Significantly. Fine-grain and lightweight litters track the most because they cling to paws and scatter easily. Medium-grain clumping clay, walnut shell, and crystal litters track the least because their larger, heavier particles fall off paws quickly.

faq.question: How do I stop my cat from tracking litter everywhere?
faq.answer: Use a three-part system: (1) an enclosed or two-room litter box, (2) medium-grain or heavy litter, and (3) a double-layer litter mat placed directly at the exit. This combination eliminates 90%+ of tracking for most cats.

faq.question: Are litter mats worth buying?
faq.answer: Absolutely. A good litter mat costs $15-25 and catches 50-70% of escaped granules. It's the cheapest upgrade you can make for tracking reduction. Look for double-layer honeycomb designs with waterproof backing.

faq.question: Can I use a top-entry box for a senior cat?
faq.answer: Generally no. Senior cats with arthritis or reduced mobility may not be able to jump in and out of a top-entry box. Use an enclosed side-entry design instead — the two-room enclosure provides similar tracking reduction without requiring jumping.
